blocks.content: He'd said the name out loud for the first time.
To someone else. To a police inspector. On a recorded call, in all probability.

He thought about what that meant. What it set in motion. The investigation would quietly tighten now — Davenport would be watching her differently, running her differently through the timeline, building the architecture of a case that would need to survive a courtroom.
Julian's job was to give Davenport enough to build on. Not to build it himself. That was the line.

She's been carrying this for eight years. Whatever Marcus did — whatever happened that she never recovered from — she made a decision and she executed it with more discipline than most people bring to anything in their lives.
That's not nothing. That's a kind of grief so heavy and so old that it became something else entirely.

Nadia was in the kitchen next door. He could see her profile through the window — making tea, or coffee, moving with the quiet efficiency she brought to everything. She looked like a woman on a Tuesday morning at a beach house. She looked like someone's friend.
She's also someone who came here with piano wire and a plan and waited for the right moment to use both.
He turned away from the window. Went back to his legal pad.
He turned the page back over.
